text:
An Australian Olympic equestrian medallist has vowed to get back on the horse for the Olympic Games in Paris after a horror fall left him in the ICU.

Australian three-time Olympic equestrian medallist Shane Rose has vowed to get back on his horse after a horror fall from the saddle during training left him in intensive care with multiple broken bones.

Rose, 50, woke up with no memory of the accident after having qualified for his fourth Olympic Games.

Falling on March 14, Rose suffered a broken femur, fractures to his pelvis, broken ribs, a broken elbow and a severe concussion.

He was put into intensive care and could only recall faintly riding the horses in the morning of the dreadful accident which threatens his prospects of competing in France in late July.

Speaking to A Current Affair alongside his wife Niki Rose, who is also a horse rider, Rose said he had “some pretty negative thoughts” during his time in hospital.

“Apparently I rode four of my more experienced horses and I have no recollection of jumping any of them or how it happened,” he said.

“The ambulance trip, getting to the hospital, talking to doctors, nothing.”

One of the horses he was riding, named Bandit, is believed to have hit a jump pole and had fallen onto him.

Rose’s wife said two people had heard it because he had hit the jump “quite hard”.

“One of the girls looked in that direction and saw the horse coming back and the horse was fine,” she said.

Rose said while in hospital during the first few days he felt “average” as he was in pain and had been frustrated he could not sleep.

“I couldn’t roll over,” he said.

“I tried to get the nurse to help me try and lie on my side and that was excruciating.

“I just broke down really. But it’s been good, the next morning I had a shower which was great. It’s amazing, a little shower can make you feel so much better.”

On the upside, Rose said he did not fear riding again because he had no recollection of the moment that would have left him traumatised.

Rose made headlines last month after a complaint was lodged against him forwearing a mankini during a fancy dress competition.

An anonymous complaint against Rose was made by an attendee of the Wallaby Hill Extravaganza competition in the NSW Southern Highlands on February 11 when he donned the rather revealing costume.

Rose apologised for causing offence and claimed on Channel Nine’s A Current Affair on Monday night the stunt was intended "to have a bit of fun".

In a statement, Equestrian Australia accepted his apology and after reviewing the incident confirmed he had not breached their code of conduct, but announced it would assess their minimum dress rules for future events.

Rose won silver in team eventing, a competition resembling an equestrian triathlon, during the 2008 and 2020 Olympics, while taking home bronze during the 2016 Games.

The Equestrian events will be held at the Palace of Versailles between July 27 and August 6, with team and individual events in eventing, dressage and jumping.

title: Aussie Olympic equestrian medallist determined to ride at Paris 2024 after breaking bones in horror fall from horse | Sky News Australia
description: Australian three-time Olympic equestrian medallist Shane Rose has vowed to get back on his horse after a horror fall from the saddle during training left him in intensive care with multiple broken bones.
